prompt_md: A class header file should contain which of the following?

options.a: The class definition with member declarations, protected by an include guard or `#pragma once`
options.b: The full implementations of all member functions, including multi-line ones
options.c: Only `#include` directives and no class content
options.d: The `main()` function along with the class definition

explanation_md: A header file holds the class definition (member names, types, and function declarations) plus an include guard to prevent double-inclusion. Multi-line function bodies go in the `.cpp` implementation file. `main()` belongs in a `.cpp` file, never in a header.

prompt_md: When defining a member function *outside* the class body in a `.cpp` file, what must appear before the function name?

options.a: Nothing — just write the function name as usual
options.c: The class name followed by `::` (scope resolution operator)

explanation_md: Without `ClassName::`, the compiler treats the function as an ordinary free function unrelated to the class. The scope resolution operator `::` tells the compiler this definition belongs to `ClassName`, matching the declaration in the header.

options.a: The class definition will be processed twice, causing a redefinition error
options.b: `Widget::show` is defined in the header outside the class body with no `inline` keyword, causing a multiple-definition linker error when two translation units include the header
options.c: The header is missing `#include <iostream>`, causing a compile error

explanation_md: Both problems apply. Without an include guard, the class definition is seen twice in any file that includes the header more than once, causing a redefinition error. And even with an include guard, defining `Widget::show` outside the class body in the header (without `inline`) gives each including `.cpp` its own copy of the function, producing a multiple-definition linker error when the program is linked.

prompt_md: Why are member functions defined *inside* the class body (in the header) safe to include from multiple `.cpp` files?

options.a: They are automatically marked `static`, giving each translation unit its own copy
options.b: The compiler ignores redefinitions for class members
options.c: Functions defined inside a class body are implicitly `inline`, which allows multiple identical definitions across translation units
options.d: Header files are not compiled separately, so there is only one definition

explanation_md: The C++ standard permits multiple identical `inline` function definitions across translation units as long as they are identical. Member functions defined inside the class body are implicitly `inline`, which is why short getters or one-liners are safe to put directly in the header.

`Temp` is constructed with `val = 7`. `show()` prints `val * 2`, which is `14`. In a single-file setup the class definition and its inline member function are both in the same translation unit, which is perfectly valid.
